segments.text: We've got Stevenson here.
Um, Anna Gold, nice to see you, Phil. We're in your hands.
You've got 10 minutes
as an organization, so we look forward to it.
No problem at all. So, uh, I'd like to thank the committee
for hearing our submission today.
Um, we've also got in the audience, uh, Rachel McKinney
and Paul Wheel and from Oceana Gold, and,
and I'm a, a partner at Deloitte representing them
as their tax advisor.
So our submission deals with a gap in the application
of the investment boost rules on mining infrastructure.
So the type of infrastructure I'm talking about, roads,
dams, tunnels, et cetera.
And the reason why we are submitting verbally today is
that it's a bit easier to sort of talk through the why.
Uh, we consider there's a bit of a gap in the rules
and verbally rather than in writing.
And I think I'll just start by setting the same.
So as we all know,
investment Boost provides an immediate 20% deduction on
assets that are commissioned in New Zealand.
Now, mines are a little bit different
to some other industries in that we have two types
of assets in a mine, we have our plant
and equipment, which is depreciated under ordinary tax rules
and eligible for investment boost.
So that's all very well understood.
And we also have our mining assets
and that's the, the infrastructure of the mine.
So predominantly land improvements, the roads, dams, tunnels
that I talked about earlier.
Now those assets are capitalized
and they're amortized over the life of the mine.
That, um, cost base of the mining
assets comes from two places.
So it comes from expenditure
that is incurred on assets built
after the decision has been made
to enter into commercial production.
Now that expenditure is capital expenditure
goes straight to balance sheet.
It's called mining development expenditure,
and it's currently eligible for investment boost.
But what we also have in our mining assets is there is also
expenditure on assets that are built
before the decision is made
to enter into commercial production.
So that's during what's called the exploration
phase of the mine.
Now, the expenditure on those assets
during the exploration phase is initially deductible in full
because there's uncertainty
around whether they will end up being a long
term asset or not.
If the mine's not viable, then
that deduction becomes permanent,
but if the mine is viable, so in other words,
the decision is made to box on with commercial production,
then that deduction's reversed.
The expenditure is treated as as income
and it is added to the cost base of the mine
and amortized over the life
of the mine in exactly the same way that that post, um,
decision to enter commercial production development
expenditure is amortized.
The difference though, uh,
from an investment boost perspective is that while
that expenditure is treated the same
as development expenditure, it is not included
in the definition of development expenditure and
accordingly is not included as a new investment asset
for the purposes of investment boost.
So it's not eligible for investment boost.
Now, the important thing here is
that there's actually no distinction between the types
of assets that are built during the exploration phase
and during the development phase.
The only distinction is the time of the mines lifecycle
that the expenditure hasn't occurred.
So just coming back to that road dams, tunnels example, so
the claw back expenditure that forms part
of the mining assets,
that is money spent on building these long term assets.
And the only reason why there's an a deduction immediately
is because it may be that the mine's not viable,
so there's no long-term benefit from it.
Uh, but the way that the rules work is they do recognize
that if, um, miners box on and,
and develop their mine, that those assets will be used
over the life of the mine.
And so accordingly it would be inappropriate for them
to have got a deduction in full right from the start.
A another way to put it is that if we didn't have this
specific provision that allowed deductibility,
it would just be capital expenditure
and when the asset was commissioned,
it would be eligible for investment boost.
It's just that there's a step in between that, um,
allows a deduction first.
So sort of to, to sum it all up, you know, um, our,
our submission just says that it's logical
that investment boost should apply to assets
that arise from clawback exploration expenditure in the
year that it's clawed back.
And that's for three reasons.
First of all, the money is spent on an
asset and that's important.
The second thing is that money spent on the same type
of asset post commercialization would be eligible
for investment boost.
And the third reason is that
because we are saying
that investment boost would only be able
to be claimed when the expenditure is called back,
that's consistent with the policy intent of investment boost
that you can only claim it when you commission the asset.
And that's the point that the, um, assets commissioned.
So that sort of, I suppose, sets out the why, uh,
behind our submission and,
and thank you to the committee for hearing it.

ABA Charitable Trust is a New Zealand registered charity

So why does this matter? At its heart?

will meet regularly with them

Funding is project specific
